speaker: interviewer
text: So you are now in the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how are you treated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- in Camp Kilmer, well, people were very nice. And everybody try to get his papers and the connection with their relatives or friends to get out from the camp and to go somewhere. So that's what we did. We-- we telegraph-- sent a telegraph to-- so then-- yeah, that was in Camp Kilmer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Ilona's sister who lived in New York came out to see us. And it was we're going to stay in New York. But a friend of ours from-- not a friend-- Ilona's cousin came up from New Jersey. And he-- she invited us to stay with them in New Jersey, in Linden. So she took us to her home. And we were there for maybe two weeks. Then we could get an-- an apartment. And we moved to our own apartment there. And Ilona is working. And I got a job. I was working. And-- What was your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: My job-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We arrived on a Wednesday. On a  Thursday evening, the next day Thursday evening, a friend of Ilona's cousin came over to see us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We couldn't understand one word English.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Ilona's relative spoke not very well Hungarian. But this friend of them spoke Yiddish. And I was speaking Yiddish with them. And what do you want to do? I want to work something. What you like to do? Anything, anything. So he said, well I have a friend of here on Elizabeth-- Elizabeth Avenue. Maybe I can't get you a job over there. Next morning I'll pick you up. That was Friday morning. He picked me up and take over to this place. This place was where there was 28 people were working over there. They were manufacturing ironing board. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So the boss said, well, you can come in. I can pay you $1.25. That was the-- the-- the-- the limit, the-- on how much they pay. He said $1.25. Fine. So Monday I was there and I was working at there. My job was four little end pieces to welding together. I was standing by a machine. And with my legs I was driving the machine with my legs. That was my job. How long did it take you before you learned English?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I always-- we never went to school. We didn't have time to go to school. We were working always. And it was just we picked up. We picked up during those years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your wife, Ilona, is she a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your-- your wife is a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Ilona is a survivor, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your children today, Henry and Marta, what do they-- what is their profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Henry is a professor of computer science. And he lives in North Carolina, Chapel Hill. He has a very nice position. And Marta, she lives in Albany, in Northern California, next to Berkeley. And she is a-- a family counselor, family and marriage counselor. And she has her own office. And Henry has two children. And Marta has two children. And they live happily, working hard, busy, very busy.

speaker: interviewer
text: The names of your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Henry's two children, [? Sammy, ?] who is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] eight year old. [? Miriam ?] is six year old. Marta's children, Jacob, he's 13, going to be bar mitzvah. And they-- their little girl, [? Sophie, ?] is eight year old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you come to Pasadena?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were in-- in New Jersey for a couple of months. And in '58 summer, we came to Pasadena. Ilona had her sister here in Pasadena. And the thing, we really wasn't very happy there  in New Jersey. First of all because it was always raining. It was always the wind. And there was the weather. We didn't-- we didn't like the weather over in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Ilona's sister, who lived here in Pasadena always wrote, why don't you come to Pasadena? It's so nice over here. And one day we decided, yes, we go to Pasadena. So we packed all our things, and we came to Pasadena by train. And what was your work here?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your job here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I arrived in Pasadena. It was a Wednesday. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then I spoke, a little bit I spoke English and understand English. I read the paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I find a paper by my wife's sister, and there was an ad. They were looking for a screen man. That's what I did in New Jersey.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh I did the-- the-- in New Jersey this ironing board. That I did for only for two weeks. And then I switched to another job where we made window screen and aluminum windows, aluminum doors.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I found an ad in the paper. They was looking for aluminum screen worker. And I went over there. And I applied for the job. And I got it. I was working over there. And that's how you made a living here in Pasadena?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's how I made a living in Pasadena. Then I worked over there a couple months. And by an accident, a work related accident, I had a hernia. I went to the doctor. Now, this job was a small job. Only two, three people were working over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went to the doctor with a hernia. Now, you have some insurance or compensation? Yes, I worked over here. Once you get the name of your insurance or compensation or the number and everything. So I ran back to the work-- the-- the boss, and I asked him, doctor want this. He said we don't have any insurance. By the way, he was a very mean man, very. Anyway, I go to the doctor. We don't have any insurance. Don't have it. So the doctor have to report to the whatever, don't have any insurance. So we have to go to the court. And they fined him. Of course, when I came back and I went back to the doctor with no insurance, he fired me right away. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the case went to the court. They fined him for $1,000 and they have to pay the hospital and the surgery for me. That cost him, at that time, another $1,000. But this took about three months. I didn't look for another job because I didn't want to have another job. I want to do my own work. I bought some tools and some screen materials. And I went door to door. I'm a screen maker.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I asked him, need some screens? I'm [? very ?] able. I install and do this and this. I went door to door. I don't know eight, 10, until finally I got some work. So I made up these screens, or the doors, whatever were the material in my garage. I made up a big table where I could work and make up the screens for the screen doors and deliver and install. So you built your own business?

speaker: interviewee
text: My own business. And installed the screens and the doors. And I got my money. I bought some new materials. I went again door to door. I got-- when I got my-- some-- some work, I-- I made it-- I made up the work, delivered, and installed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of your business?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no name that business that time. I was-- I was just trying to-- to-- to how it's going to work. And when it's going to work, and I'm going to ask for a business license. And-- so that was about two, three weeks I was working that. And one day while I was delivering and install screen, somebody from the city hall showed up. I heard somebody reported to city, there is something going on here. There is some-- some people working here, some screen seller. And I said, no, no, there's nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he didn't look around Of course, I'm thinking nothing, fine, nothing. Then I was afraid working over there in the garage. The landlord lived in the same-- the same-- well in another building over there. He didn't mind. He allowed me to work in the garage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I-- then I rented a building. I-- I-- at that time, not a whole building, a room where I can work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry, and-- sorry to interrupt you. But, so eventually you had your own business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name-- and the name of your business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aluminum Screen and Door Products Company.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you still work in that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was retired, sold the business in '82. I was working for 24 years in that business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you do today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I am retired and do volunteer work. And I do around the house. There is a lot of work around the house. And do gardening.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you want to tell your story? Why did you want to tell your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why I wanted to tell this story? You asked me, and I told you this story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you believe in God today? Do you believe in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I believe, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why I survived? That's always, from the beginning when I survived, that was an open question. I never could find the answer. Why me from my whole family, why only me? I couldn't get the answer for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your experience affect your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I believed in God always. And I believed God helped me to survive. And God helped me to-- to find the right woman. And God helped me to have the wonderful children what I have so much naches from them. And everything I believe it's that God helped me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what message would you like to leave to your children and grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'd like that my children would stay Jews, and be honest, help people, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and be friendly to people, and whenever-- wherever needs some help and they can help, they should help people, and stay Jewish, practice the Jewish tradition, remember how they grew up, where they come from, where the grow up, remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you would like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you are the one who is supposed to ask questions. [LAUGHS] And I answer the questions. If you have anything else you would like to ask, I'm be glad to answer.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's it. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. This is my mother. Her name is Ide [INAUDIBLE]. And this picture original made when she was married to my father around 1890. And from a little picture, Ilona, my wife, had a painter, that's an oil painting. It was in 1947. This is my brother's wedding picture. His name is William Fuchs, Vilmos, and her bride [? Ella ?] [INAUDIBLE]. And made in Tokaj.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after the war, I found it somewhere. I can't remember where I get this picture. Now, this picture is my older sister with her husband Samuel Katz and their son Imre. This picture was made about 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister name is Ilonna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ilonna Fuchs Katz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In [INAUDIBLE] in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- how did you get a hold of the this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when-- when I came home, in the garbage I found many pictures. Probably I-- it-- it was from that. Because there was-- our house was ransacked. I didn't find anything, any furniture, absolutely nothing was there for the garbage piles. In the garbage pile, I find some pictures. Probably that where this come from too. This picture made approximately 1935. But my older sister's children, Ilonna's children, Imre, the oldest one, Marta, and Laci.

speaker: interviewer
text: Last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Katz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in [INAUDIBLE], where they lived. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Those are the same children what we saw before. Imre, holding the baby Laci. And the little girl is Marta Katz. This little boy is Pali Friedlander, the son of my younger sister Miriam. It's made around 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In [INAUDIBLE], in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we don't have a photo of your sister Miriam?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't have a picture of her, no. This is a picture of myself, Morton Fuchs. [INAUDIBLE] It's made around 1935 in Tokaj in Hungary. This picture made in the forced labor camp in 1940, April. The-- from left, the fourth one, myself. And next to me there-- there on the picture on the fifth one, it's my brother, Vilmos Fuchs. It's made in the labor camp in [INAUDIBLE], Hungary. This is my family house. My father build it approximately 19-- 1892. I was a born in this house in 1911. And also my son Henry was born in this house, my Henry Fuchs, in 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now the picture was taken in 1990. '90-- 1990? In 1990, we were-- '90-- yeah we were home. Now this picture made on our 50 wedding anniversary, in 19-- 1996, February. And at top, from left, is our son Henry Fuchs. Next to him his wife [? Melanie ?] [? Mintzer. ?] Next is Marta Fuchs [? Winik, ?] our daughter, and her husband Fred [? Winik. ?] On the bottom picture is [? Sammy ?] trying to hide behind me, and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Morton Fuchs. And this little girl, [? Miriam. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Fuchs, and Ilona my wife. And Jacob, my daughter's-- Marta's son.

speaker: interviewer
text: Last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jacob [? Winik ?] and [? Sophie ?] [? Winik, ?] Marta and Fred's daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture made on our wedding anniversary in 1996, February in Pasadena, California. This picture shows Kubini Zoltan with his wife. Kubini Zoltan was my last commanding officer in the forced labor camp where I served for five years.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was given on last year, personally, to my daughter Marta by their son Martin Kubini.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the year this photograph was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know that. Oh, when were taken? When I think that's where the-- it was taken when they were married in Budapest, Hungary. I don't know the year.
